name: Rocket Fuel
summary: Rocket Fuel was a programmatic ad-buying DSP that went public in 2013 but its stock collapsed amid slowing growth, and it was acquired by Sizmek for $145M in 2017, a fraction of its IPO valuation.
lesson: A successful IPO doesn't guarantee durability if the core ad-tech margins erode under competition.
source: TechCrunch, AdExchanger coverage of Rocket Fuel/Sizmek acquisition 2017

name: Sizmek
funding: publicly traded, spun off from Digital River 2016
summary: Sizmek, an ad-serving and DSP platform spun off from Digital River, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2019 and sold its assets to Amazon and Vector Capital.
lesson: Legacy ad-serving businesses can collapse quickly once buyers consolidate around fewer, more integrated platforms.
source: Reuters, AdExchanger Sizmek bankruptcy coverage 2019

name: Videology
summary: Videology, a video advertising software company, filed for bankruptcy in 2018 after a planned acquisition by Amobee fell through, and its assets were later sold off in pieces.
lesson: A collapsed acquisition deal can be fatal for a cash-constrained company with no fallback financing.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of Videology bankruptcy 2018

name: Millennial Media
summary: Millennial Media, a mobile ad network that IPO'd in 2012, saw its valuation crater amid competition from Facebook and Google and was acquired by AOL in 2015 for about $238M, far below its peak market cap.
lesson: Ad networks without proprietary inventory or data are vulnerable when platform giants enter the same market.
source: TechCrunch, Fortune coverage of AOL-Millennial Media acquisition 2015

name: MediaMath
summary: MediaMath, an early independent demand-side platform, shut down operations in 2023 after running out of cash and failing to secure rescue financing or a sale.
lesson: Independent ad-tech infrastructure players face existential risk when scale economics favor a few dominant platforms.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday MediaMath shutdown coverage 2023

name: Turn Inc
summary: Turn Inc, a programmatic DSP, was sold to Amobee in 2017 for a reported fraction of the capital it had raised after struggling to compete with larger ad-tech platforms.
lesson: Heavily funded ad-tech middlemen can be squeezed out once bigger integrated platforms undercut them on price and data.
source: AdExchanger coverage of Turn/Amobee acquisition 2017

name: aQuantive
funding: public company, acquired by Microsoft for $6.3B in 2007
summary: Microsoft acquired the digital marketing conglomerate aQuantive for $6.3 billion in 2007 to compete with Google, then wrote off the entire value of the deal in 2012 after failing to integrate or monetize it.
lesson: Overpaying for a strategic acquisition without a clear integration plan can destroy the value entirely.
source: New York Times, Reuters coverage of Microsoft aQuantive writedown 2012

name: Atlas Solutions
funding: acquired by Microsoft 2007 as part of aQuantive, sold to Facebook 2013
summary: Ad-serving platform Atlas was acquired by Facebook in 2013 to build cross-device ad measurement, but Facebook shut the product down in 2017 as it consolidated ad tools internally.
lesson: Acquired ad-tech products can be discontinued once the acquirer's internal roadmap changes, regardless of the product's quality.
source: Digiday, AdExchanger coverage of Atlas shutdown 2017

name: Klout
summary: Klout, which scored social media influence, was acquired by Lithium Technologies in 2014 and ultimately shut down in 2018 after failing to build a sustainable business model around its scores.
lesson: A viral engagement metric doesn't automatically translate into a durable monetizable product.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Klout shutdown 2018

name: Massive Incorporated
summary: Massive Incorporated built an in-game advertising network and was acquired by Microsoft in 2006 for around $200M, but Microsoft shut the unit down in 2010 after the in-game ad market failed to scale as projected.
lesson: Emerging ad formats can be acquired at a premium before the underlying market has proven it can scale.
source: TechCrunch, Reuters coverage of Massive Inc shutdown 2010

name: IGA Worldwide
summary: In-game advertising network IGA Worldwide struggled to generate sufficient advertiser demand and sold its assets in 2009 during the broader collapse of the in-game ad sector.
lesson: New ad inventory formats need proven advertiser demand, not just novel supply, to sustain a standalone business.
source: VentureBeat, GamesIndustry.biz coverage of IGA Worldwide 2009

name: comScore
summary: Digital measurement firm comScore restated years of financial results after an accounting scandal involving its non-cash trade transactions, wiping out most of its market value and triggering leadership turnover and lawsuits.
lesson: Aggressive non-cash revenue recognition practices can unravel a media measurement company's credibility overnight.
source: SEC filings, Wall Street Journal coverage of comScore accounting scandal 2016-2017

name: Cambridge Analytica
summary: Political marketing and data firm Cambridge Analytica shut down in 2018 after revelations it harvested Facebook user data without consent for political ad targeting, triggering global regulatory backlash.
lesson: Data-targeting businesses that skirt consent rules face existential regulatory and reputational risk when exposed.
source: The Guardian, New York Times Cambridge Analytica investigation 2018

name: Adconion Media Group
summary: Global ad network Adconion Media Group had several executives criminally charged by the US Department of Justice for wire fraud related to hijacking IP address blocks for ad inventory, severely damaging the company before its assets were sold off.
lesson: Ad-tech supply-side fraud, even at the executive level, can permanently taint a company's ability to operate.
source: DOJ press release, AdExchanger coverage of Adconion IP hijacking case 2018

name: Ve Interactive
summary: UK-based marketing technology firm Ve Interactive collapsed into administration in 2018 after aggressive overexpansion into dozens of countries outpaced its revenue.
lesson: Rapid international expansion without matching revenue growth can bankrupt an otherwise promising martech company.
source: Financial Times, TechCrunch coverage of Ve Interactive collapse 2018

name: Blippar
summary: Augmented-reality marketing platform Blippar fell into administration in 2018 after burning through over $130M without finding a sustainable revenue model, and was later sold to new owners in a restructuring.
lesson: Flashy AR marketing technology can attract huge funding rounds while masking a lack of paying-customer demand.
source: TechCrunch, Financial Times coverage of Blippar administration 2018

name: Powa Technologies
summary: Mobile commerce and marketing platform Powa Technologies collapsed into administration in 2016 after its billionaire founder's claims of a multi-billion-dollar valuation proved unsupported by actual revenue.
lesson: Valuations built on founder hype rather than verifiable revenue eventually collide with reality.
source: Financial Times, BBC coverage of Powa Technologies collapse 2016

name: Percolate
summary: Content marketing platform Percolate raised over $130M at a high valuation but was sold to Seismic in 2019 in what was widely reported as a distressed, fire-sale acquisition.
lesson: Heavily-funded enterprise martech startups can still fail if sales cycles and retention don't justify the burn rate.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Percolate/Seismic deal 2019

name: Kahuna
summary: Mobile marketing automation startup Kahuna raised tens of millions but was quietly acquired for its technology around 2021 after failing to achieve independent scale in the crowded martech space.
lesson: A crowded marketing-automation category punishes vendors without a clearly differentiated wedge.
source: Crunchbase, TechCrunch reporting on Kahuna

name: Radius Intelligence
summary: B2B predictive marketing data company Radius shut down operations in 2020 after raising over $100M, unable to find a buyer or sustainable path to profitability.
lesson: Data-driven martech platforms need a durable data moat, not just funding, to survive category consolidation.
source: TechCrunch, GeekWire coverage of Radius shutdown 2020

name: Nanigans
summary: Facebook ad-buying platform Nanigans, once a fast-growing adtech firm, shut down in 2019 after losing customers to in-house tools and larger competitors.
lesson: Point-solution ad-buying tools are exposed when platform owners and holding companies build competing in-house capabilities.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of Nanigans shutdown 2019

name: Trada
summary: Crowdsourced PPC management startup Trada shut down abruptly in 2015 after running out of funding and failing to convert its marketplace model into sustainable margins.
lesson: Crowdsourced service marketplaces in ad management face brutal margin pressure that can outweigh their cost advantages.
source: TechCrunch, Denver Business Journal coverage of Trada shutdown 2015

name: Simply Measured
summary: Social media analytics platform Simply Measured shut down in 2019 after being unable to keep pace with larger social-analytics competitors and platform API restrictions.
lesson: Analytics tools reliant on third-party platform APIs are vulnerable when those platforms tighten data access.
source: GeekWire, TechCrunch coverage of Simply Measured shutdown 2019

name: Adobe Advertising Cloud
summary: Adobe shut down its Advertising Cloud DSP business in 2023, exiting the ad-buying market entirely after years of trying to compete with Google and The Trade Desk, and laid off the associated team.
lesson: Even a large enterprise software company can fail to break into ad-tech if it can't match scale players' data and reach.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of Adobe Advertising Cloud shutdown 2023

name: Oath Inc (Verizon Media)
funding: Verizon-owned, acquired AOL for $4.4B and Yahoo for $4.5B
summary: Verizon combined AOL and Yahoo into Oath/Verizon Media hoping to build an ad-tech and media rival to Google and Facebook, but wrote down billions in value and sold the unit to Apollo Global Management in 2021 for just $5B, far below the roughly $9B it had paid.
lesson: Bundling legacy media and ad-tech assets doesn't create a duopoly competitor without genuine platform advantages.
source: Wall Street Journal, CNBC coverage of Verizon Media sale to Apollo 2021

name: C3 Metrics
summary: Marketing attribution startup C3 Metrics became embroiled in a high-profile lawsuit with WPP alleging fabricated data and credentials, effectively destroying its credibility and business.
lesson: Attribution and measurement vendors live or die by data integrity; fabrication claims can be fatal even without a final verdict.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of C3 Metrics/WPP lawsuit 2018-2019

name: Phrasee
summary: AI-powered marketing copywriting platform Phrasee shut down in 2023, citing an inability to raise further funding amid a tightening venture market and rising competition from general-purpose generative AI tools.
lesson: Narrow AI-copywriting tools can be rapidly commoditized once general-purpose large language models arrive.
source: TechCrunch, Marketing Week coverage of Phrasee shutdown 2023

name: Persado
summary: AI marketing-language platform Persado, once valued near unicorn status, was acquired by WPP in 2024 in a deal reported to be well below its peak valuation after growth stalled.
lesson: AI-driven martech valuations built during a hype cycle can compress sharply once growth normalizes.
source: Wall Street Journal, AdExchanger coverage of WPP-Persado acquisition 2024

name: Marin Software
summary: Search and social ad management platform Marin Software went public in 2013 but saw its stock collapse over the following decade as it struggled to retain customers against Google and Facebook's own tools, repeatedly restructuring to avoid delisting.
lesson: Third-party ad-management layers are structurally at risk once the underlying ad platforms build competing native tools.
source: SEC filings, AdExchanger coverage of Marin Software decline

name: 4C Insights
summary: Social and TV ad-tech platform 4C Insights shut down in 2019, selling some assets, after failing to achieve profitability in the competitive cross-channel ad measurement space.
lesson: Cross-channel measurement platforms need a clear cost advantage or proprietary data to outlast better-funded rivals.
source: AdExchanger, Chicago Tribune coverage of 4C Insights shutdown 2019

name: Chitika
summary: Contextual ad network Chitika shut down its publisher ad network in 2020 after nearly two decades, citing an inability to compete with the scale of Google's and Amazon's ad networks.
lesson: Independent contextual ad networks eventually get squeezed out as duopoly platforms capture ad-buyer budgets.
source: Company announcement, AdExchanger coverage of Chitika shutdown 2020

name: Burst Media
summary: Online ad network Burst Media wound down operations around 2014-2015 after failing to transition its display network model to the programmatic era, with remaining assets absorbed by other firms.
lesson: Ad networks built on manual sales models often fail to adapt fast enough to programmatic buying.
source: AdExchanger reporting on Burst Media wind-down

name: Reflektive
summary: People-management and marketing-adjacent analytics platform Reflektive shut down in 2021 after failing to reach sustainable unit economics despite heavy funding.
lesson: Even well-funded SaaS platforms can collapse if customer acquisition costs never converge with lifetime value.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Reflektive shutdown 2021

name: Digital Media Solutions
summary: Performance marketing company Digital Media Solutions went public via SPAC in 2020 but its stock collapsed over 90% amid heavy debt load and slowing ad revenue, leading to major restructuring.
lesson: SPAC-driven public listings in performance marketing exposed heavily-leveraged businesses to severe market corrections.
source: SEC filings, Digiday coverage of Digital Media Solutions stock collapse

name: Bazaarvoice
summary: Ratings-and-reviews martech platform Bazaarvoice went public in 2012 but was hit by an SEC accounting investigation and a poorly integrated acquisition (PowerReviews), and was eventually taken private by Marlin Equity in 2018 at a steep discount to its IPO value.
lesson: Poorly integrated acquisitions combined with accounting missteps can permanently derail a public martech company's growth story.
source: SEC filings, Reuters coverage of Bazaarvoice take-private 2018

name: Kapost
summary: Content marketing platform Kapost struggled to differentiate in a crowded market and was effectively wound down, with its technology and customers folded into Uberflip around 2019.
lesson: Content marketing platforms without a defensible niche get absorbed by consolidating competitors.
source: Crunchbase, MarTech reporting on Kapost/Uberflip

name: Scripted
summary: Freelance content-writing marketplace Scripted for marketers shut down in 2019 after failing to scale profitably against lower-cost freelance platforms and in-house content teams.
lesson: Content marketplaces face commoditization pressure that erodes margins faster than growth can compensate.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Scripted shutdown 2019

name: TapInfluence
summary: Influencer marketing platform TapInfluence was acquired by rival IZEA in 2019 in a deal widely viewed as a distressed sale after TapInfluence failed to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Early influencer-marketing platforms struggled to build durable moats in a market that consolidated quickly.
source: TechCrunch, MarTech coverage of IZEA-TapInfluence acquisition 2019

name: Adly
summary: Celebrity and influencer marketing platform Adly, an early mover in influencer advertising, shut down around 2016 after failing to build a scalable, repeatable business model.
lesson: Being an early mover in a new advertising category isn't enough without a repeatable, scalable sales motion.
source: AdWeek, Digiday reporting on Adly wind-down

name: YuMe
summary: Video advertising platform YuMe went public in 2013 but its stock fell sharply amid slowing growth, and it was acquired by RhythmOne in 2018 for about $185M, well below its earlier market valuation.
lesson: Public ad-tech companies without continued differentiation see multiple compression that leads to discount acquisitions.
source: AdExchanger coverage of RhythmOne-YuMe acquisition 2018

name: Sambreel
summary: Sambreel operated browser toolbar and ad-injection software that was banned by Facebook, Google, and browser vendors around 2013-2014 for injecting ads without proper disclosure, crippling its business.
lesson: Ad-injection business models built on deceptive practices are eventually shut out by platform policy enforcement.
source: Wired, Ars Technica coverage of Sambreel adware bans 2013-2014

name: Vringo
summary: Mobile video ringtone and ad company Vringo failed to gain consumer traction and pivoted almost entirely into patent litigation against Google and others, abandoning its original product business.
lesson: When a consumer ad product fails to find a market, pivoting to litigation is a sign the original business model has collapsed.
source: Reuters, TechCrunch coverage of Vringo's patent-litigation pivot

name: RadiumOne
summary: Ad-tech firm RadiumOne's IPO plans collapsed in 2014 after CEO Gurbaksh Chahal was fired for a domestic violence conviction, and the company never recovered its earlier growth trajectory or went public.
lesson: A founder's personal conduct scandal can derail an IPO and permanently damage an ad-tech company's momentum.
source: TechCrunch, Recode coverage of RadiumOne CEO scandal 2014

name: Nexage
summary: Mobile ad exchange Nexage was acquired by AOL's Millennial Media in 2015 for around $107.6M, and its technology was subsequently absorbed and its independent brand discontinued as Millennial Media itself was later acquired.
lesson: Mobile ad exchanges without unique inventory access got consolidated into larger holding companies rather than scaling independently.
source: AdExchanger, Mobile Marketer coverage of AOL-Nexage acquisition 2015

name: The Trade Desk
summary: The Trade Desk built an independent demand-side platform for programmatic advertising and became one of the most successful public ad-tech companies, with a market cap in the tens of billions.
lesson: An independent, transparent DSP model can thrive at scale if it aligns incentives with advertisers rather than owned media.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: Criteo
summary: French retargeting ad-tech company Criteo went public in 2013 and remains a leading independent performance-advertising platform despite increased competition and privacy changes.
lesson: A focused technical niche like retargeting, executed well, can sustain a public ad-tech company through multiple industry shifts.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: AppNexus
summary: Programmatic ad exchange and infrastructure company AppNexus was acquired by AT&T in 2018 for about $1.6 billion to form the Xandr advertising business.
lesson: Building foundational ad-tech infrastructure can create significant exit value even without going public.
source: Wall Street Journal, AdExchanger coverage of AT&T-AppNexus acquisition 2018

name: DoubleClick
summary: Ad-serving pioneer DoubleClick was acquired by Google for $3.1 billion in 2008, becoming the foundation of Google's display and ad-server business.
lesson: Owning the ad-serving layer of the internet made DoubleClick a strategically essential acquisition for a search giant expanding into display.
source: New York Times, SEC filings on Google-DoubleClick acquisition 2008

name: Invite Media
summary: Demand-side platform Invite Media, co-founded by future Trade Desk CEO Jeff Green, was acquired by Google in 2010 for about $81 million and became the basis of DoubleClick Bid Manager.
lesson: A capital-efficient early DSP can achieve an outsized exit relative to funds raised if it builds category-defining technology.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Google-Invite Media acquisition 2010

name: Admeld
summary: Publisher yield-optimization platform Admeld was acquired by Google in 2011 for roughly $400 million, strengthening Google's supply-side ad-serving offerings.
lesson: Solving the publisher yield-optimization problem well made Admeld a strategic buy for the platform controlling ad demand.
source: AdExchanger, TechCrunch coverage of Google-Admeld acquisition 2011

name: AdMob
summary: Mobile ad network AdMob was acquired by Google in 2010 for about $750 million after a competing bid from Apple, becoming central to Google's mobile advertising strategy.
lesson: Being first to solve mobile ad monetization at the dawn of the smartphone era created a bidding war and a huge exit.
source: TechCrunch, Wall Street Journal coverage of Google-AdMob acquisition 2010

name: BlueKai
summary: Data management platform BlueKai was acquired by Oracle in 2014 for approximately $400 million, becoming a cornerstone of Oracle's data cloud and marketing offering.
lesson: Being an early leader in third-party data management made BlueKai a key asset for enterprise software vendors entering ad-tech.
source: Reuters, AdExchanger coverage of Oracle-BlueKai acquisition 2014

name: Krux
summary: Data management platform Krux was acquired by Salesforce in 2016 for roughly $700 million, forming the basis of Salesforce's DMP offering within its Marketing Cloud.
lesson: A well-timed data-platform acquisition can deliver a strong multiple on invested capital when it plugs a strategic gap for a CRM giant.
source: Bloomberg, AdExchanger coverage of Salesforce-Krux acquisition 2016

name: DataXu
summary: Programmatic DSP DataXu was acquired by Roku in 2019 for about $150 million to accelerate Roku's connected-TV advertising ambitions.
lesson: Programmatic buying technology found a second life as an acquisition target for streaming platforms building CTV ad businesses.
source: AdExchanger, Variety coverage of Roku-DataXu acquisition 2019

name: Right Media
summary: Ad exchange pioneer Right Media was acquired by Yahoo in 2007 for approximately $850 million, at the time one of the largest ad-tech acquisitions.
lesson: Building the first real-time ad exchange marketplace created enormous strategic value during the early programmatic land grab.
source: New York Times, TechCrunch coverage of Yahoo-Right Media acquisition 2007

name: BrightRoll
summary: Video advertising platform BrightRoll was acquired by Yahoo in 2014 for about $640 million, becoming the backbone of Yahoo's video ad-tech stack.
lesson: Focusing early on video ad infrastructure paid off as video advertising demand surged industry-wide.
source: AdExchanger, TechCrunch coverage of Yahoo-BrightRoll acquisition 2014

name: Omniture
summary: Web analytics leader Omniture was acquired by Adobe in 2009 for about $1.8 billion, forming the foundation of the Adobe Analytics and Marketing Cloud businesses.
lesson: Web analytics proved to be a durable enough category that a public company could still command a premium enterprise acquisition.
source: Wall Street Journal, SEC filings on Adobe-Omniture acquisition 2009

name: Responsys
summary: Email and cross-channel marketing platform Responsys was acquired by Oracle in 2014 for about $1.5 billion as part of Oracle's marketing cloud buildout.
lesson: Established marketing-automation platforms with enterprise customer bases became prime acquisition targets during the marketing-cloud consolidation wave.
source: Reuters, TechCrunch coverage of Oracle-Responsys acquisition 2014

name: Eloqua
summary: Marketing automation pioneer Eloqua went public in 2012 and was acquired by Oracle the same year for about $871 million, becoming a core part of Oracle's Marketing Cloud.
lesson: Being an early category creator in marketing automation allowed Eloqua to command a strong valuation even shortly after its IPO.
source: Wall Street Journal, SEC filings on Oracle-Eloqua acquisition 2012

name: ExactTarget
summary: Email marketing platform ExactTarget, which had acquired Pardot, was bought by Salesforce in 2013 for about $2.5 billion, becoming Salesforce Marketing Cloud.
lesson: Marketing automation platforms with strong enterprise adoption became anchor acquisitions for CRM vendors building unified customer clouds.
source: New York Times, SEC filings on Salesforce-ExactTarget acquisition 2013

name: Marketo
funding: IPO 2013, taken private by Vista for $1.79B in 2016, sold to Adobe for $4.75B in 2018
summary: Marketing automation platform Marketo went public in 2013, was taken private by Vista Equity Partners in 2016, and was then sold to Adobe in 2018 for $4.75 billion.
lesson: A private-equity turnaround can significantly increase a martech company's strategic value before a final strategic sale.
source: Wall Street Journal, TechCrunch coverage of Adobe-Marketo acquisition 2018

name: HubSpot
summary: Inbound marketing and CRM platform HubSpot went public in 2014 and has grown into a multi-billion-dollar public company serving small and mid-sized businesses.
lesson: Building a content-and-community-led go-to-market motion can create durable competitive advantage in crowded martech categories.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: Mixpanel
summary: Product analytics platform Mixpanel has grown into a profitable, thriving independent company serving thousands of businesses without needing to IPO or be acquired.
lesson: A focused analytics product can sustain long-term independent growth without chasing an immediate exit.
source: Crunchbase, company reporting on Mixpanel growth

name: Optimizely
summary: A/B testing and experimentation platform Optimizely grew into a category leader and was acquired by Episerver (backed by Insight Partners) in 2020, continuing to operate as a leading digital experience platform.
lesson: Creating and dominating a new product category, like A/B testing, can translate into a strong long-term strategic acquisition.
source: TechCrunch, company press release on Episerver-Optimizely deal 2020

name: LiveRamp
funding: spun off from Acxiom, independent public company since 2018
summary: Identity resolution and data connectivity platform LiveRamp was spun out of Acxiom and became an independent public company in 2018, growing into a key infrastructure provider for the ad-tech data ecosystem.
lesson: Spinning off a high-growth data business from a legacy parent can unlock significant independent value.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports on LiveRamp spinoff

name: PubMatic
summary: Supply-side advertising platform PubMatic went public in 2020 and has continued to operate profitably as an independent public company serving publishers in programmatic advertising.
lesson: Focusing on the sell-side of programmatic advertising and prioritizing profitability enabled a durable public-company outcome.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: Integral Ad Science
summary: Digital ad verification and measurement company Integral Ad Science (IAS) went public in 2021 and continues to operate as a leading independent ad-verification provider.
lesson: Ad verification and brand-safety became durable, structurally necessary categories as programmatic advertising scaled.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: DoubleVerify
summary: Digital media measurement and analytics company DoubleVerify went public in 2021 and has grown into one of the leading independent ad-verification and brand-safety platforms.
lesson: Trust and verification infrastructure became indispensable as advertisers demanded transparency in programmatic media buys.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: Magnite
funding: IPO 2014 as Rubicon Project, merged with Telaria in 2020 to form Magnite
summary: Supply-side platform Rubicon Project merged with Telaria in 2020 to form Magnite, which became the largest independent sell-side ad-tech platform, later acquiring SpotX and SpringServe to expand into CTV.
lesson: Consolidation among independent supply-side platforms can create scale advantages that let survivors compete with walled gardens.
source: SEC filings, AdExchanger coverage of Rubicon-Telaria merger 2020

name: Moat
summary: Ad measurement and analytics company Moat was acquired by Oracle in 2017 for a reported $850 million, strengthening Oracle's Data Cloud measurement capabilities.
lesson: Building trusted, independent measurement technology can command a premium acquisition price from enterprise data platforms.
source: AdExchanger, Business Insider coverage of Oracle-Moat acquisition 2017

name: Buddy Media
summary: Social media marketing platform Buddy Media was acquired by Salesforce in 2012 for about $745 million, becoming part of the Salesforce Marketing Cloud alongside Radian6.
lesson: Riding the enterprise adoption wave of social media marketing led to an outsized strategic acquisition.
source: New York Times, TechCrunch coverage of Salesforce-Buddy Media acquisition 2012

name: Radian6
summary: Social media monitoring platform Radian6 was acquired by Salesforce in 2011 for approximately $326 million, becoming the foundation of Salesforce's social listening tools.
lesson: A capital-efficient social-listening startup can deliver an outstanding return multiple when acquired by a CRM platform building out its cloud.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Salesforce-Radian6 acquisition 2011

name: Vitrue
summary: Social media marketing platform Vitrue was acquired by Oracle in 2012 for approximately $300 million as part of Oracle's push into social relationship management.
lesson: Early social media management tools found strong exits as enterprise software vendors raced to build social marketing suites.
source: TechCrunch, Reuters coverage of Oracle-Vitrue acquisition 2012

name: Wildfire Interactive
summary: Social media promotions platform Wildfire Interactive was acquired by Google in 2012 for a reported $350 million, delivering a strong return before Google eventually discontinued the product.
lesson: A strong acquisition exit can still be a success for founders and investors even if the acquirer later discontinues the product.
source: TechCrunch, AdAge coverage of Google-Wildfire acquisition 2012

name: AdRoll (NextRoll)
summary: Retargeting and performance marketing platform AdRoll, now operating under parent company NextRoll, has grown into a thriving independent company serving tens of thousands of SMB advertisers.
lesson: Serving underserved SMB advertisers with self-serve retargeting tools can sustain profitable independent growth for over a decade.
source: Crunchbase, company reporting on AdRoll/NextRoll

name: AppLovin
summary: Mobile app marketing and monetization platform AppLovin went public in 2021 and grew into one of the largest ad-tech companies by market capitalization, driven by its AI-based ad-buying engine.
lesson: Vertically integrating ad-buying, mediation, and an app portfolio created powerful data feedback loops that competitors struggled to match.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: InMobi
summary: Mobile advertising network InMobi, based in India, grew into one of the largest independent mobile ad-tech companies globally and remains a private unicorn spanning multiple ad-tech products.
lesson: Building mobile ad-tech infrastructure focused on emerging markets can create durable scale outside the US/Europe duopoly battleground.
source: Crunchbase, TechCrunch reporting on InMobi

name: ZoomInfo
summary: B2B sales and marketing intelligence platform ZoomInfo went public in 2020 and grew into a multi-billion-dollar public company providing contact and intent data to go-to-market teams.
lesson: Owning a proprietary, continuously-updated B2B contact database created a durable moat that sustained public-market growth.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: Braze
summary: Customer engagement and marketing automation platform Braze went public in 2021 and continues to grow as an independent public company serving consumer brands' cross-channel messaging needs.
lesson: Focusing on real-time, cross-channel customer engagement for consumer apps carved out durable differentiation from legacy marketing clouds.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports

name: Klaviyo
summary: E-commerce marketing automation platform Klaviyo went public in 2023 and has grown into a leading email/SMS marketing platform tightly integrated with Shopify and other e-commerce platforms.
lesson: Deep integration with a dominant e-commerce ecosystem can fuel rapid, capital-efficient growth to a successful IPO.
source: SEC filings, company investor reports
